text: {"matches":[{"type":"narrower","fromIndex":1,"toIndex":0,"rationale":"Claim 1 adds the condition that the association applies to both caffeinated and decaffeinated coffee, making it a specific case of the broader effect described in Claim 0."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":2,"toIndex":0,"rationale":"Claim 2 describes characteristics of coffee drinkers (non‑smoking, physical activity) whereas Claim 0 reports a mortality risk reduction; they address different aspects of the same cohort and can both be true."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":3,"toIndex":0,"rationale":"Claim 3 concerns methodological uncertainty (possible residual confounding) while Claim 0 states an observed risk reduction; the statements pertain to different scopes and are not mutually exclusive."},{"type":"compatible-different-scope","fromIndex":4,"toIndex":0,"rationale":"Claim 4 reports a Mendelian randomization finding of no causal effect, whereas Claim 0 reports an observational association of lower mortality risk; the causal versus associative scopes differ, allowing both to coexist."}]}
